narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 65% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. The primary driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile—which signaled a significant drop in performance relative to others. This stood out as the most influential factor, heavily outweighing other elements in the model's eyes. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 worked in your favor, as it pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, being close to the average of 3.21 seen across applicants. Your LSAT score of 28 also nudged things slightly toward failure, since it's notably lower than the typical 36.7 average, though its impact was less dominant than your later academic ranking. While positives like your upper-middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with the norm—full-time being the path for over 90% of students—they weren't enough to offset the concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, who maintain steadier high school deciles and stronger test scores, your profile showed more variability, especially that Year 3 dip. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant with clear drive, but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the average, strengthening recent grades or explaining that Year 3 context in a personal statement, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that value resilience. Building a narrative around your upward trajectory from Year 1's solid 6th decile could also help in reapplying. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
